title: Measuring enthalpy changes
summary: Almost every reaction moves energy in or out as heat, and this lesson is where you learn to put a number and a sign on that flow. It builds the whole of R1.1: exothermic and endothermic reactions and how to read their energy profile diagrams; the enthalpy change ΔH with its sign conventions and standard conditions; measuring ΔH in the lab by calorimetry using q = mcΔT and converting a heat change into an enthalpy change per mole; and estimating ΔH from bond enthalpies as bonds broken minus bonds formed. It is a calculation-heavy topic, so it is built around fully worked examples laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards the marks.
learningObjectives: Distinguish exothermic and endothermic reactions in terms of energy transfer to or from the surroundings, and interpret their energy profile diagrams.
Define the enthalpy change ΔH, apply the sign convention (negative for exothermic, positive for endothermic), and state the standard conditions for ΔH⊖.
Measure an enthalpy change by calorimetry using q = mcΔT and convert the heat change into a molar enthalpy change in kJ mol⁻¹.
Estimate a reaction enthalpy from bond enthalpies as (bonds broken) − (bonds formed).
Present working so that method marks and error-carried-forward (ECF) protect the final answer.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Putting a Number on Heat
simpleExplanation.summary: Reactions release or absorb heat, and enthalpy is our way of bookkeeping that energy. Exothermic reactions hand energy out to the surroundings and warm them up; endothermic reactions pull energy in and cool them down. Measuring the temperature change of the surroundings, or comparing the strengths of the bonds broken and made, lets us pin down exactly how much.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a chemical reaction as a bank account of energy stored in bonds. Breaking bonds is a withdrawal — it costs energy. Making bonds is a deposit — it releases energy. If the deposits are bigger than the withdrawals, the reaction pays out the surplus as heat (exothermic, ΔH negative). If the withdrawals are bigger, the reaction has to draw energy in from its surroundings to balance the books (endothermic, ΔH positive).
steps: Decide the direction: does the temperature of the surroundings go up (exothermic) or down (endothermic)?
Measure the heat moved into or out of the surroundings with q = mcΔT.
Divide by the moles of the reactant that reacted to get an enthalpy change per mole, and attach the correct sign.
Or estimate it from the data booklet: add up the bond enthalpies of bonds broken, subtract the bond enthalpies of bonds formed.

flashcards.back: The heat content of a system at constant pressure. Its absolute value cannot be measured; only the CHANGE, ΔH, during a reaction can be measured.
The heat energy exchanged between the reaction (system) and its surroundings at constant pressure. $\Delta H = H(\text{products}) - H(\text{reactants})$, in kJ mol⁻¹.
Releases heat TO the surroundings, so the surroundings warm up. Products have lower enthalpy than reactants, so ΔH is NEGATIVE (ΔH < 0).
Absorbs heat FROM the surroundings, so the surroundings cool down. Products have higher enthalpy than reactants, so ΔH is POSITIVE (ΔH > 0).
The enthalpy change measured under standard conditions: pressure 100 kPa and a stated temperature (usually 298 K), with all substances in their standard states and solutions at 1 mol dm⁻³.

The heat transferred to the surroundings. q = heat (J), m = mass of the SURROUNDINGS being heated/cooled (usually water, in g), c = specific heat capacity (J g⁻¹ K⁻¹), ΔT = temperature change (K or °C).
The energy needed to raise the temperature of 1 g of a substance by 1 K. For water, c = 4.18 J g⁻¹ K⁻¹ (Section 2 of the data booklet).

flashcards.front: Converting q to ΔH per mole
flashcards.back: $\Delta H = \dfrac{-q}{n}$, where n is the moles of the reactant that reacted. The minus sign flips the sign because heat gained by the surroundings equals heat lost by the system. Convert J to kJ.
A graph of enthalpy (y-axis) against reaction progress (x-axis). Reactants and products sit at fixed enthalpy levels; ΔH is the vertical gap between them; the hump is the activation energy Ea.
The energy needed to break one mole of a particular covalent bond in the gaseous state. Breaking bonds is always ENDOTHERMIC (needs energy); making bonds is always EXOTHERMIC (releases energy).
$\Delta H = \Sigma(\text{bonds broken}) - \Sigma(\text{bonds formed})$. Broken first (in), formed second (out). Only an estimate, because tabulated bond enthalpies are averages and apply to gases.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: which mass in q = mcΔT?
flashcards.back: m is the mass of the SURROUNDINGS that changed temperature — usually the water — NOT the mass of the fuel or solid that reacted. Using the fuel's mass is a classic error.

quickCheck.prompt: A reaction causes the temperature of the surrounding water to RISE. The reaction is:
0.30 g of a fuel is burned to heat 150 g of water. In q = mcΔT, the mass m you must use is:
A solution cools from 25.0 °C to 18.0 °C. The value of ΔT to put into q = mcΔT is:
Using bond enthalpies, ΔH is calculated as:
On an energy profile diagram, the products sit LOWER than the reactants. This tells you the reaction is:

faq.q: Why is ΔH negative for an exothermic reaction when heat is 'released'?
faq.a: ΔH is defined from the point of view of the SYSTEM (the reactants and products), not the surroundings. $\Delta H = H(\text{products}) - H(\text{reactants})$. In an exothermic reaction the products store less energy than the reactants, so this subtraction gives a negative number. The energy that leaves the system is what warms the surroundings up — but the system itself has lost enthalpy, hence the minus sign.

faq.q: In q = mcΔT, why do we use the mass of the water and not the mass of the fuel or the solid?
faq.a: q = mcΔT measures the heat absorbed by the SURROUNDINGS, and the surroundings are what is changing temperature — normally the water. The fuel or solid is the source of the heat, not the thing being heated, so its mass never goes into mcΔT. The mass of the reactant is used separately, to work out the number of MOLES when you convert q into ΔH per mole.

faq.q: Why does a bond-enthalpy calculation only give an ESTIMATE of ΔH?
faq.a: Tabulated bond enthalpies are AVERAGE values taken across many different molecules, so the real bond in your specific compound may be a little stronger or weaker. They also strictly apply to substances in the gaseous state, so if a reactant or product is a liquid or solid the estimate ignores the enthalpy involved in changing state. That is why a bond-enthalpy value and a directly measured (experimental) value rarely match exactly.

faq.q: Why doesn't my experimental enthalpy of combustion match the data booklet value?
faq.a: Simple calorimetry loses heat to the surroundings, and combustion experiments also suffer from incomplete combustion, evaporation of the fuel, and heat carried away by the container. All of these mean less heat reaches the water than the reaction actually produced, so the measured temperature rise — and therefore the calculated |ΔH| — comes out smaller than the true value. Examiners expect 'heat loss to the surroundings' as the main reason.

sections.content: Strike a match, dissolve a fertiliser, neutralise an acid — in every case energy is quietly moving between the reaction and its surroundings as heat. The enthalpy change, ΔH, is how chemists measure that flow: its size tells you how much heat, and its sign tells you which way. This lesson gives you three ways to get at ΔH — reading it from an energy profile diagram, measuring it in the lab with q = mcΔT, and estimating it from bond enthalpies — and every worked example is laid out exactly the way marks are awarded, because in a calculation topic HOW you show your working decides how many marks you keep.
Exothermic and endothermic reactions

Chemical bonds store energy, and a reaction rearranges those bonds, so almost every reaction transfers energy to or from its surroundings. If the reaction gives out energy — the surroundings get hotter — it is exothermic. Combustion, neutralisation and most oxidation reactions are exothermic; you can feel the heat. If instead the reaction takes energy in — the surroundings get colder — it is endothermic. Dissolving ammonium nitrate, or the thermal decomposition of a carbonate, are endothermic; the flask feels cold. The key is that we judge the direction by what happens to the SURROUNDINGS, because that is what we can actually put a thermometer in.

items: **Exothermic:** heat flows OUT of the system into the surroundings. Surroundings warm up. Products have LOWER enthalpy than reactants, so ΔH is NEGATIVE.
**Endothermic:** heat flows INTO the system from the surroundings. Surroundings cool down. Products have HIGHER enthalpy than reactants, so ΔH is POSITIVE.
The system is the reactants and products; the surroundings are the solvent, the container and the air.
Whatever heat the system loses, the surroundings gain (and vice versa): $q_{surroundings} = -q_{system}$.

sections.content: Energy profile diagrams

An energy profile diagram plots enthalpy on the vertical axis against the progress of the reaction on the horizontal axis. The reactants start at one enthalpy level and the products end at another; the difference in height between them is ΔH. Between the two there is a hump — the reaction has to climb over an energy barrier before new bonds can form. The height of that barrier above the reactants is the activation energy, Ea. For an EXOTHERMIC reaction the products sit LOWER than the reactants (the line finishes below where it started, ΔH negative); for an ENDOTHERMIC reaction the products sit HIGHER than the reactants (the line finishes above where it started, ΔH positive).

items: **ΔH** is the vertical gap from the reactant level to the product level: products below reactants → exothermic (−); products above → endothermic (+).
**Activation energy Ea** is the height of the peak measured UP from the reactant level, not from the baseline.
The arrow for ΔH runs from reactants to products; the arrow for Ea runs from reactants up to the top of the hump.
A bigger hump does not change ΔH — activation energy and enthalpy change are independent quantities.

sections.question: An energy profile diagram shows the reactants at an enthalpy of 250 kJ, the peak of the curve at 400 kJ, and the products at 100 kJ (all on the same arbitrary scale). (a) State, with a reason, whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic. (b) Determine ΔH for the reaction. (c) Determine the activation energy, Ea. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a)** The products (100 kJ) lie BELOW the reactants (250 kJ), so the system loses enthalpy to the surroundings — the reaction is **exothermic**.  [A1]

**(b)** $\Delta H = H(\text{products}) - H(\text{reactants}) = 100 - 250 = -150$ kJ.
The negative sign confirms it is exothermic.  [M1 subtraction in the right order, A1 value and sign]

**(c)** Activation energy is measured from the REACTANT level up to the peak:
$E_a = 400 - 250 = +150$ kJ.  [A1]

*Note that Ea is measured from the reactants (250), not from the baseline (0) — measuring from zero is a common slip.*

sections.content: Enthalpy change ΔH: sign conventions and standard conditions

Enthalpy, symbol $H$, is the heat content of a system at constant pressure. We cannot measure the absolute enthalpy of anything, but we can measure the CHANGE that accompanies a reaction, and that is the enthalpy change, $\Delta H$. It is defined as the enthalpy of the products minus the enthalpy of the reactants, which is why the sign falls out automatically: if the products hold less energy, ΔH is negative and the reaction is exothermic.
\Delta H = H(\text{products}) - H(\text{reactants})

Because the amount of heat a reaction gives out depends on conditions such as temperature and pressure, chemists quote a STANDARD enthalpy change, written $\Delta H^{\ominus}$, measured under an agreed set of conditions so that values can be compared fairly. ΔH is always quoted per mole of reaction as written in the balanced equation, in kJ mol⁻¹.

items: **Sign convention:** exothermic ΔH < 0 (negative); endothermic ΔH > 0 (positive). Never quote an enthalpy change without its sign.
**Standard conditions (⊖):** pressure 100 kPa, a stated temperature (usually 298 K / 25 °C), each substance in its standard state, and any solutions at a concentration of 1 mol dm⁻³.
**Units:** kJ mol⁻¹, per mole of reaction as written in the balanced equation.
Doubling the equation doubles ΔH; reversing the equation reverses the sign of ΔH.

sections.content: The sign is worth marks on its own. A calorimetry answer of '56 kJ mol⁻¹' for an exothermic reaction is incomplete — it must be −56 kJ mol⁻¹. Decide the sign from the physics (did the surroundings warm up or cool down?), not from whatever sign your calculator happens to show.
Measuring ΔH by calorimetry

Calorimetry measures an enthalpy change indirectly, by measuring the temperature change of the surroundings — usually water. A simple calorimeter is an insulated container, such as nested polystyrene cups with a lid, so that the heat released or absorbed by the reaction goes into (or comes from) the water rather than leaking to the room. We measure how much the water's temperature changes and turn that into a heat quantity, q, using the relationship below.
q = mc\Delta T

Here $q$ is the heat transferred (J), $m$ is the mass of the SURROUNDINGS being heated or cooled — the water — in grams, $c$ is the specific heat capacity (4.18 J g⁻¹ K⁻¹ for water, from the data booklet), and $\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial}$ is the temperature change in K (a change of 1 °C is a change of 1 K, so either unit works). Two steps then turn q into a molar enthalpy change: first, the heat gained by the water equals the heat lost by the reaction but with the opposite sign, so $\Delta H = -q/n$; second, divide by the number of moles of the reactant that actually reacted and convert from J to kJ.

The single most common calorimetry error is putting the wrong mass into q = mcΔT. m is the mass of WATER (the surroundings), never the mass of the fuel or the solid that dissolved. The mass of the reactant is used separately — to work out the moles when you convert q into ΔH per mole. Keep the two masses in different boxes on your page.

sections.question: 50.0 cm³ of 2.00 mol dm⁻³ hydrochloric acid is mixed with 50.0 cm³ of 2.00 mol dm⁻³ sodium hydroxide in a polystyrene cup. Both start at 20.0 °C and the temperature rises to a maximum of 33.6 °C. Calculate the enthalpy change of neutralisation in kJ mol⁻¹. (Assume the mixture has the density and specific heat capacity of water: 1.00 g cm⁻³ and 4.18 J g⁻¹ K⁻¹.) [4]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — mass of the surroundings (the water).**
Total volume = 50.0 + 50.0 = 100.0 cm³, so $m = 100.0 \times 1.00 = 100.0$ g.  [use mass of solution, NOT of any solid]

**Step 2 — temperature change and heat.**
$\Delta T = 33.6 - 20.0 = 13.6$ K.
$q = mc\Delta T = 100.0 \times 4.18 \times 13.6 = 5684.8$ J $= 5.685$ kJ.  [M1]

**Step 3 — moles of the reactant.**
$n(\text{HCl}) = c \times V = 2.00 \times \dfrac{50.0}{1000} = 0.100$ mol, and the acid and base react 1 : 1, so 0.100 mol of water is formed.  [M1]

**Step 4 — enthalpy change per mole, with sign.**
The temperature rose, so the reaction is exothermic and ΔH is negative:
$\Delta H = \dfrac{-q}{n} = \dfrac{-5.685}{0.100} = -56.8$ kJ mol⁻¹.  [A1 magnitude, A1 sign and units]

*ΔT is given to 3 s.f., so −56.8 kJ mol⁻¹ (3 s.f.) is appropriate; −57 kJ mol⁻¹ is also acceptable.*

sections.content: Estimating ΔH from bond enthalpies

A reaction is really the breaking of some bonds and the making of others. Breaking a bond always COSTS energy (it is endothermic), and making a bond always RELEASES energy (it is exothermic). The bond enthalpy is the energy needed to break one mole of a given bond in the gaseous state, and the data booklet lists average values. If the bonds made are stronger, in total, than the bonds broken, the reaction releases a net surplus of energy and is exothermic. This gives a quick way to estimate ΔH without any experiment.
\Delta H = \Sigma(\text{bond enthalpies of bonds broken}) - \Sigma(\text{bond enthalpies of bonds formed})

items: **Bonds broken go first (energy in, positive); bonds formed go second (energy out, negative).** Broken minus formed.
Count every bond in the balanced equation, remembering coefficients (2 mol HCl means 2 × the H–Cl bond enthalpy).
A negative answer means the bonds formed are stronger overall → exothermic; a positive answer means the bonds broken are stronger overall → endothermic.
It is only an ESTIMATE: tabulated bond enthalpies are averages and strictly apply to gases, so the value differs from a directly measured one.

sections.question: Estimate the enthalpy change for the reaction H₂(g) + Cl₂(g) → 2HCl(g) using these average bond enthalpies (kJ mol⁻¹): H–H = 436, Cl–Cl = 242, H–Cl = 431. [3]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — bonds broken (reactants).**
1 mol H–H + 1 mol Cl–Cl $= 436 + 242 = 678$ kJ (energy IN).  [M1]

**Step 2 — bonds formed (products).**
2 mol H–Cl $= 2 \times 431 = 862$ kJ (energy OUT).  [M1]

**Step 3 — apply broken − formed.**
$\Delta H = \Sigma(\text{broken}) - \Sigma(\text{formed}) = 678 - 862 = -184$ kJ mol⁻¹.  [A1 value and sign]

*The answer is negative, so the reaction is exothermic — the two H–Cl bonds formed are stronger, in total, than the H–H and Cl–Cl bonds broken.*

sections.content: Write the calculation in the fixed order 'broken − formed' every single time. Students who set it up as 'formed − broken' get the right magnitude with the wrong sign, and the sign is where the mark is. Also make sure you have multiplied by the coefficients: forgetting the '2' in 2HCl is the other classic bond-enthalpy slip.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Dropping or reversing the sign of ΔH** — exothermic must be negative, endothermic positive. Decide the sign from whether the surroundings warmed or cooled, and always write it down.
**Using the mass of the fuel or solid in q = mcΔT** — m is the mass of the water (the surroundings) that changed temperature, not the mass of the reactant.
**Muddling ΔT** — ΔT is the CHANGE in temperature ($T_{final}-T_{initial}$), not a final temperature and not a value converted to kelvin by adding 273 (a change of 7 °C is a change of 7 K).
**Forgetting to divide by moles / not converting J to kJ** — q from mcΔT is the heat for the amount used, in joules; ΔH is per mole, in kJ mol⁻¹. Divide by n and divide by 1000.
**Bond enthalpies the wrong way round** — ΔH = bonds BROKEN − bonds FORMED, and you must multiply each bond by its coefficient in the equation.
**Measuring activation energy from the baseline** — Ea is the height of the peak above the REACTANT level, not above zero.
**Confusing q and ΔH** — q is the heat measured in the experiment (J, for the amount used); ΔH is the standardised value per mole (kJ mol⁻¹) and carries the opposite sign to q.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

This is the showcase for a calculation topic. In Paper 2 the marks are analytic: each mark is tied to a specific line of working (a method mark, M, or an answer mark, A), and — crucially — the method marks and error-carried-forward (ECF) mean a wrong number early on does not cost you every mark that follows. That is only true if the method is written down. Study how each of the four marks below is earned by a specific line.

sections.question: Burning 0.25 g of ethanol raised the temperature of 100 g of water by 12.5 °C. Calculate the enthalpy of combustion of ethanol in kJ mol⁻¹. (c = 4.18 J g⁻¹ K⁻¹) [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

**Step 1 — heat gained by the water, q = mcΔT.**
The water is the surroundings, so $m = 100$ g, $\Delta T = 12.5$ K:
$q = mc\Delta T = 100 \times 4.18 \times 12.5 = 5225$ J $= 5.225$ kJ.

**Step 2 — moles of ethanol burned.**
$M(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}) = 2(12.01) + 6(1.01) + 16.00 = 46.08$ g mol⁻¹.
$n = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{0.25}{46.08} = 5.43 \times 10^{-3}$ mol.

**Step 3 — enthalpy of combustion per mole, with sign.**
The temperature rose, so combustion is exothermic and ΔH is negative:
$\Delta H_c = \dfrac{-q}{n} = \dfrac{-5.225}{5.43 \times 10^{-3}} = -963$ kJ mol⁻¹.

So $\Delta H_c \approx \mathbf{-963\ kJ\ mol^{-1}}$ (accept −960 kJ mol⁻¹ to 2 s.f.).

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **M1 — heat calculation.** Awarded for a correct q = mcΔT using the mass of WATER (100 g) and ΔT = 12.5 K: q = 5225 J. This is a METHOD mark — it is credited for the correct process even if the arithmetic slips.
- **M1 — moles of fuel.** Awarded for n = mass ÷ molar mass using the fuel's mass (0.25 g) and M(ethanol) = 46.08 g mol⁻¹. Using the fuel's mass HERE (not in mcΔT) is exactly what the question is testing.
- **A1 — magnitude of ΔH per mole.** Awarded for dividing q (in kJ) by n to reach ≈ 963 kJ mol⁻¹. Here is the protection: **error-carried-forward** — a student who made an earlier slip but correctly computed ΔH = q ÷ n on THEIR figures still earns this mark, because it credits the method of dividing heat by moles.
- **A1 — correct negative sign and units.** Awarded for stating the value as −963 kJ mol⁻¹: the negative sign (exothermic) AND the units kJ mol⁻¹.

**'Accept answers that round correctly.'** The engine accepts −963, −960 and −963.1 kJ mol⁻¹ as the same answer, and does not demand a fixed number of decimal places in the intermediate lines — it credits the correct method and a correctly-rounded final value.

**Bottom line:** of the 4 marks, 2 are method marks that survive an arithmetic slip and the answer marks are protected by ECF. A student who writes only '−963' with no working risks losing 2–3 marks if the final number is wrong; a student who shows every line keeps the method marks regardless. (The data-booklet value is about −1367 kJ mol⁻¹; the experimental figure is smaller because of heat loss to the surroundings and incomplete combustion.)

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way — analytically, mark by mark, with ECF. So always show the method: write out q = mcΔT with the right mass, show n = m/M, show ΔH = −q/n, and attach the sign. The method marks and error-carried-forward are what protect your score when the final number slips, and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
Where this leads

Enthalpy is the first step into thermodynamics, the study of what drives reactions. From here, Hess's law lets you combine enthalpy changes you can measure to find ones you cannot; enthalpy cycles use exactly the bond-breaking and bond-making bookkeeping you met above. Later you will add entropy and Gibbs free energy to decide not just how much energy a reaction moves but whether it happens at all. The habit built here — measure the heat, divide by the moles, attach the sign — is the foundation for all of it.
